I AM Caring and Curious — Professional Development

I AMCaring and Curious
A Practical Toolkit for Information, AI, and Media Literacy in a Connected World
Professional Development
Building consensus across the AI divide by starting with what every teacher already agrees on — before the conversation about AI tools begins.
Glen Warren & Stephanie Gamache  ·  Laguna Beach Unified School District  ·  CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 · 2026
Part 1

The Diagnosis

How did we get here — and what does it have to do with AI?

Facilitator Note — 10 min

Open with the diagnosis before any mention of AI. The goal is to build a shared understanding of a problem everyone already recognizes. Ask participants: "What have you noticed changing in how students engage with learning over the past several years?" Let the room name the condition before you name it for them.

The six cards build sequentially. Walk through them in order — each one earns the next. AI enters at card 4 as an accelerant, not the origin of the problem. That framing is everything.

"The reader is being read." Students are not simply navigating information environments. They are being tracked, profiled, and served content designed to capture their attention.

01
Product over process

Schools have long rewarded completion over understanding. Output over thinking. The grade went to the product — and the process became invisible.

02
The marginalized literacies

Information, media, AI, and digital literacy are all process-based — the first casualties of a product-focused culture. They were always undervalued.

03
Doing School

Denise Pope named this a generation ago. Students learned to perform learning. Citation became compliance, not capacity. The system rewarded the performance.

04
AI industrialized the consequences

AI didn't create the condition. It made it untenable. The product — the essay, the analysis, the summary — can now be generated in seconds.

05
AI is an extraordinarily powerful tool

— and it arrived into an ecosystem already selecting against the human-centered capacities students need to use it wisely. That is the perfect storm.

06
IAM is the practical response

Not another program. Not a redesign. One embedded move. The lesson already being taught. No new curriculum required.

The Reader Is Being Read — IAM's foundational framing

Explore · Try · Possibilities
Explore

The Heart of I AM

Read the full toolkit's framing of Care, Curiosity, and Connection — the human capacities this diagnosis points back to.

Open "The Heart of I AM"
Try

Name the pattern once

Before your next lesson, ask students one question: "What have you noticed changing in how you engage with information?" Just listen.

Possibilities

A shared starting point

Picture this diagnosis opening every department meeting this year — not a mandate, a shared recognition everyone already holds.

Part 2

Meet the Teachers

Three valid positions. All three right about something. All three responding to the same underlying reality from different angles.

Facilitator Note — 8 min

Ask participants to silently identify which teacher they most recognize — in themselves or in a colleague. Don't ask them to share yet. Just let them sit with the recognition. Then read each profile aloud, slowly. The goal is not judgment — it's identification.

Critical framing: none of these teachers are wrong. Marcus is right. Diana is right. James is right. The disagreement is about tactics, not values — and that gap is much smaller than it looks from the outside.

Marcus
The Enthusiast
What they're right about

AI can deepen inquiry and prepare students for the world they're entering. Authentic, critical use of AI is a skill worth teaching.

What they're worried about

Students use AI to complete rather than think. He wants tools used well — not as a shortcut around the thinking he values.

Their teaching move
AI as a starting point, not an endpoint.
Diana
The Doubter
What they're right about

Deep thinking, patience, and independent reasoning are being eroded. The habits of mind she has spent her career building are under threat.

What they're worried about

AI in student hands creates shortcuts that bypass the thinking she values most. Her resistance is rooted in care, not fear.

Their teaching move
Protect the habits of mind that make learning real.
James
The Cautious Adopter
What they're right about

AI is powerful. Students aren't ready yet. Preparation and readiness matter more than speed of adoption.

What they're worried about

Putting powerful tools in unprepared hands creates vulnerability, not capability. He wants to move carefully and intentionally.

Their teaching move
Readiness first. One small step at a time.
Explore · Try · Possibilities
Explore

Why I AM

See how the toolkit was built to hold Marcus, Diana, and James at once — and how it compares to other frameworks teachers may already know.

Open "Why I AM"
Try

Notice your own voice

This week, notice which of these three teachers you sound most like in one real conversation about AI. Just name it to yourself.

Possibilities

One team, three strengths

Picture Marcus, Diana, and James on the same grade-level team — each contributing their strength to one shared plan instead of three competing ones.

Part 3

The Common Ground

What Marcus, Diana, and James all share — before the conversation about AI tools begins.

Facilitator Note — 6 min

This is the pivot point of the entire PD. Name it explicitly: "Everything we've looked at so far — the diagnosis, the three teachers — has been building to this moment. The disagreement about AI is real. But it's smaller than what you all share."

Ask the room: "Which of these four things do you personally care most about?" Let a few people answer. You'll hear Marcus, Diana, and James all speak — and they'll all say the same things in different words.

Student Wellbeing

Students are navigating environments designed to capture their attention and shape their emotions — often without realizing it. Every teacher in the room knows this is happening.

Connected World Responsibilities

Students need to understand the impact their own creations and communications have on others. Identity, privacy, and wellbeing matter to every teacher regardless of their position on AI tools.

The Information Environment

Students are already in it — regardless of what their teacher believes about AI. Every search, every feed, every recommendation is shaping what they see and believe. The challenge exists before any tool enters the room.

Students Accepting Without Questioning

All three have seen students take outputs at face value — AI-generated, algorithmically-sorted, or otherwise. None of them are okay with it. This is the shared wound that makes the room possible.

Part 4

Meet the Students

Same classroom. Same assignment. Three different relationships to learning.
9th Grade World History — Propaganda Unit — Teacher: Unassigned

Facilitator Note — 10 min

Ask: "Which student do you see most often in your classroom?" Give people 30 seconds to think, then ask for a show of hands. Usually the room splits across all three. That's the point — these aren't rare cases. These are the students in front of every teacher right now.

After the profiles, pause on the common ground. The phrase "all three are being read" often lands hard. Let it sit. Don't rush to the next section.

Jordan
The Confident User

Fast, fluent, gets things done. Used AI to generate an analysis of a WWII propaganda poster. Technically correct. Missed the emotional manipulation entirely. Neither Jordan nor the grade noticed.

The real risk

Confident without being critical. Can't see through the tools.

IAM entry point
Analyze visual rhetoric — what choices were made, and what effect do they produce?
Amara
The Anxious Avoider

Thoughtful, careful, overwhelmed. Read everything. Couldn't trust her own analysis enough to write it down. Submitted late with an apology note.

The real risk

Anxiety without agency. No framework for what she's feeling.

IAM entry point
Curiosity as a practice — the willingness to move toward understanding rather than away from it.
Diego
The Compliant Performer

Reliable, pleasant, invisible. Turned everything in on time. Cited sources correctly because the rubric required it. Wrote a competent paragraph that revealed nothing about what he actually thought.

The real risk

Performing learning. The system can't see what's missing — and neither can he.

IAM entry point
Trace claims to original sources — making the process visible and his own.

All three are being read. All three lack agency — not because they are incapable, but because nobody has explicitly taught them that agency is possible.

All three need one adult who helps them see what is happening around them.

That adult is in this room.

Explore · Try · Possibilities

Essential Learnings, Grade 9

Find your own grade level in the full K–12 progression and see the three focus points students are working toward.

Open the Essential Learnings table
Try

Name one student silently

Pick one student in your next class who reminds you of Jordan, Amara, or Diego. As you plan tomorrow's lesson, hold their IAM entry point in mind.

Possibilities

A wall students recognize themselves in

Every grade level building its own version of this profile — students seeing themselves reflected, not being diagnosed by someone else.

Part 5

The Value Map

Nine elements. Three teachers. Their lesson. Their students. Every element has something for Marcus, Diana, and James.

Facilitator Note — 8 min

Don't read every cell aloud. Instead, ask participants to scan the table and find the cell that most directly speaks to their own teaching situation. Give them two minutes. Then ask: "Who found something that surprised them?" That conversation is usually rich.

The goal of this section is for every teacher in the room to see themselves — and their students — somewhere in this map. No element is a hard sell for any teacher. That's the architecture of consensus.

The IAM Architecture — Three Layers, Nine Elements

Layer 1 — Converging Literacies (Grade 9 Propaganda Unit)

Element Marcus — The Enthusiast Diana — The Doubter James — The Cautious Adopter
Trace claims to original sources
Information		 AI is a starting point. Tracing shows students AI outputs need verification — exactly the critical use he wants. This is precisely what she's been teaching. The framework names and elevates what she values. No AI required in student hands. Pure investigation. A safe first move.
Analyze visual rhetoric
Media		 Opens discussion of how AI-generated images use the same rhetorical choices as human-made ones. Visual analysis is the kind of deep, slow, human thinking she wants to protect. This is her territory. Concrete, analyzable, manageable. Students look at two images and think carefully. That's it.
Evaluate privacy trade-offs
AI		 Connects AI tools students already use to real economic and ethical stakes. Makes AI literacy personal. Addresses the surveillance dimension of AI without requiring AI tools in the classroom. Frames AI as a system with trade-offs — not a ban, not an endorsement. Balanced entry.

Layer 2 — Human-Centered Capacities

Element Marcus Diana James
CareCare for truth grounds responsible AI use — students who care ask better questions.Care is the antidote to passive consumption. It's what she's always tried to build.Gives his 'not ready yet' concern a name. Students need to care before they can use tools wisely.
CuriosityCuriosity prevents AI from becoming a shortcut. It keeps students in the driver's seat.Curiosity is the discipline she's been protecting. This names it precisely.One safe first move: ask students to be curious about where information comes from.
ConnectionConnection makes AI literacy transferable — students see patterns across tools and subjects.Connection is why deep reading matters. Skills that don't transfer are just performance.Connection is low-risk and high-value. Students connect claims to evidence. No AI needed.

Layer 3 — Connected World Responsibilities

Element Marcus Diana James
Identity & PresenceStudents who understand identity in connected spaces use AI more intentionally.The erosion of authentic voice is her core concern. This addresses it directly.Framing AI as a presence question — not a ban question — opens the conversation.
Privacy & AgencyData as labor reframes AI tools as economic exchanges students can evaluate.Privacy education without AI tools in the room. Completely in her comfort zone.Clear, concrete, actionable. Students understand what they give and get.
Wellbeing & IntentionalityIntentional use is what he wants. This gives students the language for it.Balance and intentionality are her values. The framework names them as learnable.Wellbeing is the shared concern. Every parent and teacher agrees students need this.

Open the Teaching Companion
Open the Assessment Guidance
Try

Choose one, not nine

Pick a single Layer 1 element and try it Monday, using the Teaching Move exactly as written. Resist the urge to do all nine at once.

Possibilities

A full year, built one element at a time

All nine elements. All three layers. A full-year scope and sequence built from small moves already proven in your own classroom.

Part 6

Meet the Parents

They don't identify with a parent type. They identify with their child.

Facilitator Note — 6 min

This section is often where teachers feel the most emotionally located. Many of them are also parents. Ask: "Which of these situations do you see most in your school community?" The bridge column is the most important — it's the answer to every parent who has said "I can't help with this."

The key message: IAM questions require no content knowledge. Any parent can ask "who made this?" in any language. That's not a literacy program — that's a human capacity.

The Content Gap

The student whose work has moved beyond what the parent can help with. Content is complex. The parent wants to help but doesn't know the material. They feel increasingly locked out as grade level rises.

The bridge home
IAM questions require no content knowledge. Any parent can ask: who made this? Why? Can we check it?
The IEP Student

Assignments are modified but the information environment isn't. The student navigates the same complex digital world as everyone else — with fewer tools and support already stretched thin.

The bridge home
The IAM "I can" statements can be adapted as measurable IEP goal language. Entry at instructional level, not grade placement.
The Language & Cultural Barrier

Homework comes home in a language the parent can't fully read, on content they can't fully access. This parent is not absent — they're doing everything they can.

The bridge home
IAM questions work in any language. These are human information skills, not English-language literacy skills.

Every parent wants their child to be okay.
That is the common ground that makes every bridge possible.

Explore · Try · Possibilities
Explore

Equity & Access

Read the full section on language, entry point, and low-tech access — including guidance built for exactly these three families.

Open "Equity & Access"
Try

Translate one bridge home

Before your next conference, translate one "bridge home" message into a family's home language. Send just that one line.

Possibilities

One question, every language

A version of every family newsletter that carries one IAM question home — in every language your families speak.

Part 7

Try It

Experience the framework as a learner first. Before you teach it — feel it.

Facilitator Note — 15 min

This is the experiential core of the PD. Do NOT explain the framework before running the activity. Teachers need to feel it before they see it named.

The activity: Show two photographs of the same historical event from different sources (any major WWII or Cold War propaganda event works well). Give participants 8–10 minutes to analyze the visual rhetoric — the angle, lighting, framing, subject selection, and emotional effect of each image. Ask them to work individually first, then share briefly with a partner.

After the activity: Run the three debrief questions below in order. Let the room answer each one before moving to the next. Then — and only then — reveal the completed Teacher Move Sheet sample. They will read it differently now.

The facilitator will show two photographs of the same historical event from different sources.

Analyze visual rhetoric — the angle, lighting, framing, and subject selection in each image. For 10 minutes, you are Jordan, Amara, or Diego. Not a teacher evaluating a framework. A student inside one.

1
What did you just do?

Name the thinking. Not the content — the cognitive move you made. Was it analysis? Comparison? What did you have to slow down and actually look at?

2
What thinking did it require?

How does that feel different from passive consumption? What would a student who did this well have demonstrated — that wouldn't show up on a typical rubric?

3
Could you see that happening in your classroom?

Specifically. In which lesson. With which students. Jordan, Amara, or Diego?

The Sample Teacher Move Sheet

Read this now with different eyes — not as a planning template, but as a description of something you just felt. See how the 8th grade teacher protected what already worked, and identified one intentional change.

Part 8

What's Next

Now pick up the toolkit with teacher eyes. Small Move. Big Impact. Start with the lesson. Strengthen the learning.

Facilitator Note — 20 min

Give each instruction separately, with working time between each. Resist the urge to explain what comes next — let teachers complete each step before seeing the next one. The sequence matters.

Timing: Step 1 — 5 min. Step 2 — 7 min (have the Teaching Companion open or printed). Step 3 — 10 min (have the Assessment Guide available). Close with 2–3 teachers sharing just the bottom two lines aloud. That's the close of the PD.

1

What are students already doing?

Describe a real lesson that's coming up. Not your best lesson. Not a lesson you're proud of. A real lesson. The framework comes to your lesson — not the other way around.

Protect what already works. — 5 minutes
2

Which IAM opportunities naturally fit?

Open the Teaching Companion to your grade level. Read the entries that feel relevant. Choose one, two, or three elements that fit naturally into the lesson you just described. You are not expected to use all nine.

The one that fits right now — not the most impressive one. — 7 minutes
3

My Small Moves + Assessment Guide

What will students do? How will this strengthen learning? Open the Assessment Guide for the element you chose. Read the "I can" statement and performance indicator. One question: how will I know the student got it?

Observable thinking — not a rubric score. — 10 minutes

Your Teacher Move Sheet

Complete the blank sheet. The two most important lines are at the bottom: what will stay the same, and what is the one intentional change. Those two lines are what you share with the room.

One element.  ·  One existing lesson.  ·  One observable indicator.  ·  Monday morning.
"A student with Care, Curiosity, and Connection can navigate anything."
That is what IAM is for.
I AM Caring and Curious: A Practical Toolkit for Information, AI, and Media Literacy in a Connected World
